text_original: If one does not preserve the learned in a state he will be injuring the state; if one is not zealous (to recommend) the virtuous upon seeing one, he will be neglecting the ruler.
text_normalized: If one does not preserve the learned in a state he will be injuring the state; if one is not zealous (to recommend) the virtuous upon seeing one, he will be neglecting the ruler.

text_original: Enthusiasm is to be shown only to the virtuous, and plans for the country are only to be shared with the learned.
text_normalized: Enthusiasm is to be shown only to the virtuous, and plans for the country are only to be shared with the learned.

text_original: Few are those, who, neglecting the virtuous and slighting the learned, could still maintain the existence of their countries.
text_normalized: Few are those, who, neglecting the virtuous and slighting the learned, could still maintain the existence of their countries.

text_original: Formerly Lord Wen (of Chin 780-746 b.c.) was once in exile and yet later became the leading feudal lord.
text_normalized: Formerly Lord Wen (of Chin 780-746 b.c.) was once in exile and yet later became the leading feudal lord.

text_original: Lord Huan (of Ch'i 685-643 b.c.) was once forced to leave his state and yet later became a tyrant among the feudal lords.
text_normalized: Lord Huan (of Ch'i 685-643 b.c.) was once forced to leave his state and yet later became a tyrant among the feudal lords.

text_original: Lord Kou Chien of Yiieh (496-465 b.c.) was once brought under humiliation by the king of Wu and yet he was later looked upon with awe by the princes of China.
text_normalized: Lord Kou Chien of Yiieh (496-465 b.c.) was once brought under humiliation by the king of Wu and yet he was later looked upon with awe by the princes of China.

text_original: The reason that these three men became famous and successful in the world lies in that they were able to endure shame and humiliation within their states,-The greatest men know of no defeat.
text_normalized: The reason that these three men became famous and successful in the world lies in that they were able to endure shame and humiliation within their states,-The greatest men know of no defeat.

text_original: The next greatest turn failure into success, and this, by the employment of the people.
text_normalized: The next greatest turn failure into success, and this, by the employment of the people.

text_original: I have heard it said: It is not that there is no peaceful abode but that I have no peaceful heart (over others’ home-lessness); it is not that my wealth is not sufficient but that my passion yearns for more (to improve others’ conditions).
text_normalized: I have heard it said: It is not that there is no peaceful abode but that I have no peaceful heart (over others’ home-lessness); it is not that my wealth is not sufficient but that my passion yearns for more (to improve others’ conditions).

text_original: Therefore the superior man is strict with one’s self but lenient with others (in matters of conduct) while the multitude are lenient with themselves but strict with others.
text_normalized: Therefore the superior man is strict with one’s self but lenient with others (in matters of conduct) while the multitude are lenient with themselves but strict with others.

text_original: The superior man carries out his ambitions successfully in action and studies the situation when he is at leisure.
text_normalized: The superior man carries out his ambitions successfully in action and studies the situation when he is at leisure.

text_original: Even when he is taken as a mediocre individual he feels no dissatisfaction.
text_normalized: Even when he is taken as a mediocre individual he feels no dissatisfaction.

text_original: This is because he has self-confidence.
text_normalized: This is because he has self-confidence.

text_original: Therefore, those who attempt what seems difficult to them -will obtain what they desire, but few who aim at what they desire can avoid what they dislike, Therefore, artful ministers are harmful to the lord and flattering subordinates are injurious to the ruler.
text_normalized: Therefore, those who attempt what seems difficult to them -will obtain what they desire, but few who aim at what they desire can avoid what they dislike, Therefore, artful ministers are harmful to the lord and flattering subordinates are injurious to the ruler.

text_original: The lord should have uncompromising ministers; the ruler should have stern subordinates.
text_normalized: The lord should have uncompromising ministers; the ruler should have stern subordinates.

text_original: Only when counsel is given with far-sightedness and advice administered with sternness, can the life of the state be secure and permanent.
text_normalized: Only when counsel is given with far-sightedness and advice administered with sternness, can the life of the state be secure and permanent.

text_original: If (to the contrary) the subordinates should value their positions and keep silence, the ministers near at hand would be speech-less and those far away could only sigh, and the people would become bitter.
text_normalized: If (to the contrary) the subordinates should value their positions and keep silence, the ministers near at hand would be speech-less and those far away could only sigh, and the people would become bitter.

text_original: When the ruler is surrounded with praises and flatteries and insulated against good counsels, then the country is in danger.
text_normalized: When the ruler is surrounded with praises and flatteries and insulated against good counsels, then the country is in danger.

text_original: Was it not because they would not employ the scholars, that Chieh and Chow lost their empire and their lives?
text_normalized: Was it not because they would not employ the scholars, that Chieh and Chow lost their empire and their lives?

text_original: Thus it is said: To offer the greatest treasure of the country to the ruler is not as laudable as to recommend the virtuous and introduce the learned.
text_normalized: Thus it is said: To offer the greatest treasure of the country to the ruler is not as laudable as to recommend the virtuous and introduce the learned.

text_original: Among the five weapons the sharpest will be broken first.
text_normalized: Among the five weapons the sharpest will be broken first.

text_original: Among the five swords the keenest will be first worn out.
text_normalized: Among the five swords the keenest will be first worn out.

text_original: The sweet wells become sooner dry and the elegant trees are oftener felled.
text_normalized: The sweet wells become sooner dry and the elegant trees are oftener felled.

text_original: The tortoises that are more responsive are oftener burned and the snakes that show more magic power are more sacrificed.
text_normalized: The tortoises that are more responsive are oftener burned and the snakes that show more magic power are more sacrificed.

text_original: Thus, Pi Kan died of his up-rightness; Meng Pen perished by his strength; Hsi Shih paid with her life for her beauty; and Wu Ch"i was torn alive for his achievement.
text_normalized: Thus, Pi Kan died of his up-rightness; Meng Pen perished by his strength; Hsi Shih paid with her life for her beauty; and Wu Ch"i was torn alive for his achievement.

text_original: This shows that there are but few who excel other people and do not perish on account of it.
text_normalized: This shows that there are but few who excel other people and do not perish on account of it.

text_original: Hence the saying: Position of the supreme is hard to keep.
text_normalized: Hence the saying: Position of the supreme is hard to keep.

text_original: Even the kind ruler will not show favours to ministers without merit.
text_normalized: Even the kind ruler will not show favours to ministers without merit.

text_original: Even the affectionate father will not love his useless sons.
text_normalized: Even the affectionate father will not love his useless sons.

text_original: He who occupies a position but is not equal to the task is not the proper person for the position.
text_normalized: He who occupies a position but is not equal to the task is not the proper person for the position.

text_original: He who draws emoluments but does not deserve the rank is not the proper proprietor of the emoluments.
text_normalized: He who draws emoluments but does not deserve the rank is not the proper proprietor of the emoluments.

text_original: Good bows may be hard to draw, but they can reach great heights and pierce deeply.
text_normalized: Good bows may be hard to draw, but they can reach great heights and pierce deeply.

text_original: Good horses may be hard to ride on, but they can carry heavy burdens and make long journeys.
text_normalized: Good horses may be hard to ride on, but they can carry heavy burdens and make long journeys.

text_original: Real talents may be hard to command, but they can be trusted to be envoys to the court of the emperor and to meet the nobility.
text_normalized: Real talents may be hard to command, but they can be trusted to be envoys to the court of the emperor and to meet the nobility.

text_original: Therefore the big rivers do not despise the little brooklete for tributaries.
text_normalized: Therefore the big rivers do not despise the little brooklete for tributaries.

text_original: And great men do not neglect any menial task or reject any trifle, and so they become vessels for the world.
text_normalized: And great men do not neglect any menial task or reject any trifle, and so they become vessels for the world.

text_original: The water in a river does not come from a single source, neither is the fur coat that is worth a thousand yi is not from the white fur of a single fox.
text_normalized: The water in a river does not come from a single source, neither is the fur coat that is worth a thousand yi is not from the white fur of a single fox.

text_original: Now, to discard those who agree with the right but employ those who agree with one s self is not the way to be a great ruler.
text_normalized: Now, to discard those who agree with the right but employ those who agree with one s self is not the way to be a great ruler.

text_original: (Just as) Heaven and earth do not dazzle, great bodies of water do not boil and foam, and great conflagrations do not coruscate, (so) the imperial character does not lift itself up beyond reach.
text_normalized: (Just as) Heaven and earth do not dazzle, great bodies of water do not boil and foam, and great conflagrations do not coruscate, (so) the imperial character does not lift itself up beyond reach.

text_original: As to the chieftain of only a thousand people, he is straight like an arrow and smooth like a hone, unable to tolerate the manifold ways.
text_normalized: As to the chieftain of only a thousand people, he is straight like an arrow and smooth like a hone, unable to tolerate the manifold ways.

text_original: For narrow gorges dry up rapidly, shallow streams are soon exhausted, and the barren land does not bear fruits.
text_normalized: For narrow gorges dry up rapidly, shallow streams are soon exhausted, and the barren land does not bear fruits.

text_original: When a ruler confines his favours within his palace, then they cannot be shared by the whole country.
text_normalized: When a ruler confines his favours within his palace, then they cannot be shared by the whole country.
